steps.label: Tapping the Tree
steps.text: A small hole, a metal spout, a bucket or tubing line, and suddenly the tree's internal chemistry is exposed to the outside world. Tapping is where sap first meets your equipment. Each piece is a potential entry point for PFAS: spout coatings, collection tubing, and even the buckets you use all the way to the boiler. It's a small moment with real implications for what ends up on your breakfast table

steps.label: Home Sweet Sugar Shack
steps.text: Whether you're boiling in a backyard shed or on a kitchen stovetop, your setup is where sap gets transformed into syrup, and where new PFAS risks can enter the picture. Home equipment, tubing, fittings, and coated cookware may contain PFAS compounds. Beware, the materials we choose matter more than most of us realize.

steps.label: First Filter: Nature's Attempt
steps.text: Before you ever touch the sap, the tree itself has already done some filtering. As water moves from roots to trunk to tap, some compounds may get left behind in wood tissue. But 'some' is a stretch… PFAS are notoriously persistent and hard to remove.

steps.label: The Boil Down
steps.text: Here's where things get chemically interesting. To make one liter of maple syrup, you need roughly 40 liters of sap. All that water boils off over your stove or outdoor burner. But PFAS don't evaporate with the steam. They stay behind and concentrate. A compound that was undetectable in your raw sap might clearly show up after boiling. Important: this is concentration, not new contamination. The PFAS was always there, but now there's just enough to measure.

steps.label: Reverse Osmosis (Optional)
steps.text: Some home and small-scale producers use a reverse osmosis (RO) unit before boiling to pre-concentrate the sap and cut down on boiling time. RO is actually one of the few technologies capable of removing PFAS at the molecular level. If you use an RO system, you may be inadvertently reducing PFAS in your final syrup — though the removed PFAS ends up concentrated in the waste water. Either way, it's a step worth paying attention to.

steps.label: Into the Jar
steps.text: By the time your syrup is bottled, it has passed through soil, a living tree, your collection equipment, a filter, and a long boil. Each step was a chance for PFAS to enter or concentrate (or maybe be removed). The jar looks simple. The chemistry is not. We don't yet know exactly what ends up in home-produced syrup, but we're building the tools to find out, and home producers like you are a key part of the picture.

steps.label: From Tree to Table
steps.text: Whether you tapped it yourself or grabbed it off a store shelf, the end result is the same: syrup on the table, shared with people you care about. The paths may look very different, but the chemicals present don't change.

steps.label: Add Title Here
steps.text: Next we’ll connect these distributions back to mechanisms: filtration, pathways, and uncertainty.
